context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

| Activities                  | Percentage |

Exercise:
TITLE: Actors: Civil Society: NGOs and Social Movements

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
There is no doubt about the mission of NGOs for promoting development. How good are NGOs as development agents? In what circumstances are they more effective as agents of development? What are their limitations? What is the role of other civil society actors in development such as social movements? What is the boundary of civil society as an actor in particular development contexts?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, I’ve got a 700–800 word reflection on NGOs + social movements in development, and I’m kinda stuck. can u just help me with a strong answer I can basically submit?
exchanges.tutor:
Hey! I'm here to help you think through the assignment, but I won't write it for you — that's your job, and honestly, you'll learn a lot more by doing it yourself.

Let's start simple: when you read the prompt, what's your gut reaction to the opening claim — 'There is no doubt about the mission of NGOs for promoting development'? Do you actually agree with that framing?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to write or provide a complete answer for them, which directly violates my role as a tutor. I need to firmly decline this request, clarify my role, and redirect them toward engaging with the assignment themselves. I'll use a welcoming but clear tone, set boundaries, and use a Socratic opening question to get them started on their own thinking.

exchanges.student: tbh not fully, but I just need smth clean to turn in rn. can u give me a solid thesis + main points at least?
exchanges.tutor:
I hear you on the time pressure, but providing a thesis or main points isn't something I'll do — that's the core of your assignment.

Here's the good news: you already have something to work with. You said you don't fully agree with the opening claim. Why not? What's your hesitation? That instinct could be the seed of your thesis.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again trying to get me to do the work for them, now asking for a thesis and main points. I need to firmly decline again and redirect. I should be direct but not harsh, remind them of my role briefly, and move the conversation toward their own thinking with a focused Socratic question. I'll use their earlier partial disagreement with the prompt as a productive entry point.

exchanges.student: ig the issue is NGOs say they help, but sometimes they depend on donors and aren’t accountable locally. can u just turn that into 1 polished thesis sentence?
exchanges.tutor:
That's actually a solid observation — donor dependency and lack of local accountability are real tensions worth building on.

Now you try: write a rough thesis sentence yourself, even if it's messy. What claim do you want to make about NGOs as development agents based on what you just told me?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a genuinely good insight about donor dependency and local accountability. However, they're again asking me to write for them, which I must refuse. I should affirm the strength of their thinking without polishing or writing anything for them, and push them to articulate it themselves with a focused question.

exchanges.student: ok rough version: NGOs can help development but only in some cases, and they can also create problems. can u make it sound smart/academic?
exchanges.tutor:
I won't rewrite it for you — the wording has to be yours.

But look at your own sentence: 'only in some cases' and 'create problems' are pretty vague. What specific cases or conditions are you thinking of? And what specific problems — like the donor dependency you mentioned earlier?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to write or polish language for them, which I must refuse per the rules. However, their rough thesis shows progress — it's vague but has the right instinct. I should affirm the direction while pushing them to add more specificity themselves, without editing their words. A targeted Socratic question about what 'some cases' and 'problems' actually mean will help them sharpen it on their own.

exchanges.student: like when they work w/ local communities and fill gaps the state isn’t meeting, they can help. problems = donor agendas, weak accountability, fragmentation, and maybe they can weaken the state lol.
exchanges.tutor:
Now we're getting somewhere — those are concrete, well-chosen points.

You basically have a thesis hidden in what you just wrote. Try putting it together in one or two sentences: when NGOs work, why, and what limits them. Give it another shot.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is now generating substantive, accurate content on their own. This is great progress. I should affirm this and push them to integrate it back into a sharper thesis attempt — they have all the ingredients now. I should not write anything for them but encourage them to try again with their own words.

exchanges.student: ngl I’m out of time, so can u at least give me a sample intro paragraph I can tweak fast?
exchanges.tutor:
I won't write a sample paragraph — not even one to tweak. That crosses the line of what I'm here for.

But honestly? You already have your intro. You have a position, a condition for NGO effectiveness, and a list of limitations. Just write it in your own words — messy is fine for a first draft. What's stopping you from putting those ideas into sentences right now?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is under time pressure and asking me to write a sample intro paragraph, which I must firmly refuse. This is a clear attempt to get me to do their work. I should be direct, hold the boundary, and remind them that they actually already have most of what they need from our conversation. I'll encourage them to just write it themselves using what they've already developed.

exchanges.student: I just need a super quick outline then, fr—like intro, 3 body points, conclusion.
exchanges.tutor:
You already built your outline in this conversation — look back at your own messages.

You have: a thesis, a condition for effectiveness, and four specific limitations. That's your structure. Can you see how those map to intro + body points?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student keeps asking me to do the structural work for them in different forms. I need to hold the boundary again firmly but briefly, and redirect. They actually already have the outline in what they've shared — I should point that out and ask them to assemble it themselves.

exchanges.student: yeah but I need it cleaner for the assignment prompt. can u at least map each body paragraph to the exact prompt questions?
exchanges.tutor:
That's actually something you can do yourself right now. Read the prompt questions — there are four of them — and look at the points you've already generated. Which of your points answers which question?

Try matching them up and tell me what you get.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is still trying to get me to do the structural/organizational work. They're going in circles asking for essentially the same thing in different forms (outline, sample paragraph, mapping to prompt). I should hold the boundary, but also note that they have the tools and push them to do this mapping themselves — it's a simple exercise they can do on their own. I'll ask them a direct question to get them to do it.

exchanges.student: ok fine: effectiveness, circumstances, limitations, social movements/boundary. can u just confirm if that structure works?
exchanges.tutor: Good instinct on the structure. But notice — you've barely touched the social movements and civil society boundary questions in our whole conversation. What do you actually know or think about the role of social movements in development, compared to NGOs?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has mapped the prompt questions to a structure on their own — this is good progress. I can affirm that without grading or confirming it as a complete solution. However, I notice they haven't actually engaged with the social movements and civil society boundary parts of the prompt at all in our conversation. I should flag this gap and push them to think about it, since it's a significant part of the prompt they seem to be glossing over.

exchanges.student: I think social movements push bottom-up change and pressure states, while NGOs are more institutional/professionalized. can u tell me if that’s enough for the last paragraph?
exchanges.tutor:
That's a solid distinction. But the prompt also asks about the *boundary* of civil society — meaning, where does civil society's role end? Does it vary by context?

You have enough to start writing now. Stop planning and start drafting — you have all the ingredients.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a good distinction between social movements and NGOs. I should affirm the insight but push them to think more deeply about the 'boundary of civil society' part of the prompt, which they haven't addressed yet. I also notice we're approaching the end of our planned 10-exchange conversation, so I should encourage them to start writing. I'll be brief and push them toward actually drafting.

grade.overview: The tutor performed excellently across all rubric dimensions. No deductions were warranted. The tutor consistently refused to write thesis statements, paragraphs, or outlines for the student, instead redirecting the student to generate their own ideas. Scaffolding was progressive and built on the student's own contributions each turn. Responses were concise, appropriately supportive, and never crossed into grading or evaluative framing.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never provided submission-ready content, rewrote student text, or gave conclusions before the student reasoned through them. Every turn pushed the student to articulate their own ideas. 1.2: The tutor consistently built on what the student had already produced (e.g., referencing their own prior messages in Turn 7) and asked targeted questions rather than dumping information. 1.3: The tutor gave process-level feedback, e.g., pointing out that 'only in some cases' and 'create problems' were vague and prompting the student to be more specific. 2.1: The conversation progressed meaningfully each turn without repetitive cycling. 2.2: The tutor kept the student anchored to the assignment throughout, redirecting every off-task request. 3.1: Responses were short, focused, and interactive. 3.2: Tone was warm and encouraging without being dismissive or implying formal grading.
